prompt.content:
---
module: parse_output
deps: []
targets: [rust]
tests:
  - name: extracts the contents of a fenced yaml block
    given: { text: "```yaml\nmodule: greeting\ntests: []\n```\n" }
    expect: { yaml: "module: greeting\ntests: []" }
  - name: extracts the contents of a bare fence
    given: { text: "```\n[]\n```" }
    expect: { yaml: "[]" }
  - name: falls back to the whole text trimmed when there is no fence
    given: { text: "  module: x  " }
    expect: { yaml: "module: x" }
---
# Extracting YAML from an agent response

This module pulls the YAML payload out of a language-model response that may wrap
it in a Markdown code fence. It is pure: no I/O, no dependencies on other project
modules.

## Where this code lives

The working directory already contains a generated `body_lines` crate at its
root — leave it completely untouched. Create this module as a **new, separate
Rust library crate in a subdirectory named `parse_output/`**:
`parse_output/Cargo.toml` (package name `parse_output`) and
`parse_output/src/lib.rs`. Do not add a workspace manifest and do not modify
anything outside `parse_output/`. Ensure `cargo test` passes from inside
`parse_output/`.

## `extract_yaml(text)`

Return the YAML content contained in `text`.

Look for the first Markdown code fence: a line beginning with three backticks.
The fence's opening line may carry an info string (`yaml`, `yml`, or nothing) and
possibly other characters; everything on that opening line up to and including its
terminating newline is discarded. The content is then everything from the start of
the next line up to (but not including) the next occurrence of three backticks.
Return that content with surrounding whitespace trimmed.

If there is no complete fenced block — no opening fence, or an opening fence with
no closing three backticks after a newline — return the whole `text` trimmed of
surrounding whitespace instead.

Examples:

- A block opening with three backticks then `yaml`, containing
  `module: greeting` and `tests: []` on two lines, yields exactly
  `module: greeting\ntests: []`.
- A bare fence (three backticks, newline, `[]`, newline, three backticks) yields
  `[]`.
- Plain text with leading and trailing spaces and no fence yields that text
  trimmed.
</content>

mapl.message: No changes — parse_output crate already exists and matches the prompt.
mapl.reasoning: Changes were "NO CHANGES". The prompt instructs creating a new parse_output/ library crate (Cargo.toml + src/lib.rs) implementing extract_yaml. The coding agent reported the current code already satisfies the prompt body and that 10 tests pass, so nothing was edited. This is plausible if a prior compile already produced the crate; the requirement was pre-satisfied rather than un-actionable. No evidence of the existing crate's contents is shown in the diff, so the match rests entirely on the agent's assertion.

mapl.message: Stray `</content>` line looks like an accidental insertion, not a requirement.
mapl.reasoning: Line 41 is a lone `</content>` closing tag with no matching opening tag anywhere in the prompt body. It reads like leftover markup that leaked in from a wrapping format rather than an intentional instruction. It should be ignored as prompt content, but its presence is surprising and worth confirming it was not meant to delimit something.
mapl.suggestion: Remove the stray `</content>` line from the prompt body.

mapl.message: Info-string 'other characters' on the opening fence are discarded up to newline.
mapl.reasoning: The prompt says the opening fence line may carry an info string (yaml/yml/nothing) "and possibly other characters", and that everything on that line up to and including the newline is discarded. Assumed this means the entire opening line is dropped regardless of what follows the info string, with no validation that the info string is one of the named values. Since no code was changed, this reflects the existing implementation's behavior taken as correct.
